verses.text: And Samuel said unto all Israel, Behold, I have hearkened unto your voice in all that ye said unto me, and have made a king over you. And now, behold, the king walketh before you: and I am old and grayheaded; and, behold, my sons are with you: and I have walked before you from my childhood unto this day. Guidance: Samuel begins his farewell with a careful framing. He notes three facts: he has done what they asked (a king is installed), the king is now visibly leading them ('walketh before you'), and Samuel himself is stepping back ('old and grayheaded'). The mention of his sons is poignant — chapter 8 began with them as a reason for the people's discontent; here he simply puts them on record as still living among the people, neither defended nor denounced. The pattern of his life is the credential: 'I have walked before you from my childhood.' Boy at Shiloh, judge at Ramah, prophet at Mizpeh, kingmaker at Gilgal — one continuous walk before God's people. Leadership transitions land cleanest when the outgoing leader names what changed and steps aside without sulking.

The mention of his sons is poignant — chapter 8 began with them as a reason for the people's discontent; here he simply puts them on record as still living among the people, neither defended nor denounced. The pattern of his life is the credential: 'I have walked before you from my childhood.' Boy at Shiloh, judge at Ramah, prophet at Mizpeh, kingmaker at Gilgal — one continuous walk before God's people. Leadership transitions land cleanest when the outgoing leader names what changed and steps aside without sulking.

Behold, here I am: witness against me before the LORD, and before his anointed: whose ox have I taken? or whose ass have I taken? or whom have I defrauded? whom have I oppressed? or of whose hand have I received any bribe to blind mine eyes therewith? and I will restore it you. And they said, Thou hast not defrauded us, nor oppressed us, neither hast thou taken ought of any man's hand. And he said unto them, The LORD is witness against you, and his anointed is witness this day, that ye have not found ought in my hand. And they answered, He is witness. Guidance: Before he hands over the keys, Samuel calls a public audit. The questions echo Moses' words at the end of Numbers 16 ('I have not taken one ass from them') — a deliberate citation; Samuel is positioning himself as the legitimate heir of the Mosaic prophetic line. Note the five categories: oxen and asses (theft of livestock), defrauding and oppressing (financial and political abuse), and bribery 'to blind mine eyes' (judicial corruption — the exact sin his sons were accused of in 8:3). He is clean on every count, and the people confirm it on oath. 'The LORD is witness, and his anointed is witness' — Saul is now part of the testimony chain. Public office should leave a paper trail clean enough to stand audit; few leaders manage it.

And Samuel said unto the people, It is the LORD that advanced Moses and Aaron, and that brought your fathers up out of the land of Egypt. Now therefore stand still, that I may reason with you before the LORD of all the righteous acts of the LORD, which he did to you and to your fathers. When Jacob was come into Egypt, and your fathers cried unto the LORD, then the LORD sent Moses and Aaron, which brought forth your fathers out of Egypt, and made them dwell in this place. And when they forgat the LORD their God, he sold them into the hand of Sisera, captain of the host of Hazor, and into the hand of the Philistines, and into the hand of the king of Moab, and they fought against them. And they cried unto the LORD, and said, We have sinned, because we have forsaken the LORD, and have served Baalim and Ashtaroth: but now deliver us out of the hand of our enemies, and we will serve thee. And the LORD sent Jerubbaal, and Bedan, and Jephthah, and Samuel, and delivered you out of the hand of your enemies on every side, and ye dwelled safe. And when ye saw that Nahash the king of the children of Ammon came against you, ye said unto me, Nay; but a king shall reign over us: when the LORD your God was your king. Guidance: Samuel runs the whole national history in seven verses. The pattern is the cycle of Judges in miniature: God delivers → people forget → God sells them into enemy hands → people cry out → God sends a deliverer. The list of deliverers — Jerubbaal (Gideon), Bedan (probably Barak), Jephthah, and 'Samuel' (himself, third person, modest) — covers the major judges. Then comes the punchline: 'when ye saw Nahash came against you, ye said, Nay, a king shall reign over us — when the LORD your God was your king.' He locates the source of the demand for kingship: not failed judges, but failed faith. The previous cycles always ended in 'cry out and be delivered.' This time they short-circuited the cycle and demanded a human structure instead of crying out. Trusting an institution to do what only the LORD does is the chronic temptation of God's people in every generation.

Now therefore behold the king whom ye have chosen, and whom ye have desired! and, behold, the LORD hath set a king over you. If ye will fear the LORD, and serve him, and obey his voice, and not rebel against the commandment of the LORD, then shall both ye and also the king that reigneth over you continue following the LORD your God: but if ye will not obey the voice of the LORD, but rebel against the commandment of the LORD, then shall the hand of the LORD be against you, as it was against your fathers. Guidance: 'The king whom ye have chosen' — and yet 'the LORD hath set a king over you.' Both are true. The people's bad ask and God's sovereign installation are not two different acts; they are one act under two descriptions. Then comes the covenantal if/then that defines the monarchy: the throne is not an exemption from the covenant but a participant in it. Both 'ye and also the king' continue together if both fear the LORD; both fall together if either rebels. This is the great innovation of Israel's kingship: the king is under the LORD's law, not above it. The same nation that wanted a king to be 'like other nations' is told plainly — your king will not be like other kings. He will be a covenant member with a crown, not a divinity in a palace.

Now therefore stand and see this great thing, which the LORD will do before your eyes. Is it not wheat harvest today? I will call unto the LORD, and he shall send thunder and rain; that ye may perceive and see that your wickedness is great, which ye have done in the sight of the LORD, in asking you a king. So Samuel called unto the LORD; and the LORD sent thunder and rain that day: and all the people greatly feared the LORD and Samuel. Guidance: Samuel asks for a sign that nature itself would refuse to grant: thunder and rain in the middle of wheat harvest — late May or early June in Israel, when rain is unheard of and would ruin the standing grain on the threshing floors. The agricultural backdrop is essential: a wheat-harvest storm is not a weather inconvenience; it is an economic catastrophe in slow motion. God obliges instantly. The natural order itself testifies that something is wrong. 'All the people greatly feared the LORD and Samuel' — they wanted a king to take the place of fearing the LORD, and the LORD gives them a sign that He is not so easily displaced. When God speaks through events you cannot ignore, He is being kind, not cruel; the alternative is to leave you happily wrong.

And all the people said unto Samuel, Pray for thy servants unto the LORD thy God, that we die not: for we have added unto all our sins this evil, to ask us a king. And Samuel said unto the people, Fear not: ye have done all this wickedness: yet turn not aside from following the LORD, but serve the LORD with all your heart; And turn ye not aside: for then should ye go after vain things, which cannot profit nor deliver; for they are vain. For the LORD will not forsake his people for his great name's sake: because it hath pleased the LORD to make you his people. Guidance: The thunder works. The people who were so confident about their political demand are now begging Samuel not to let them die. They name their request a 'sin' for the first time, and they ask the right person to pray. Samuel's response is one of the most pastoral lines in the Old Testament: 'Fear not.' Yes, you have sinned. No, you do not need to despair. The remedy is not panic but renewed obedience: 'turn not aside… serve the LORD with all your heart.' Then comes a sentence to memorize: 'The LORD will not forsake his people for his great name's sake.' Your security does not rest on the cleanness of your record, but on the costliness of His reputation. He has staked His name on you; He will not abandon what He has named. This is the gospel logic before the gospel — God keeps His people because His name is wrapped up in them, not because they have earned the keeping.

And all the people said unto Samuel, Pray for thy servants unto the LORD thy God, that we die not: for we have added unto all our sins this evil, to ask us a king. And Samuel said unto the people, Fear not: ye have done all this wickedness: yet turn not aside from following the LORD, but serve the LORD with all your heart; And turn ye not aside: for then should ye go after vain things, which cannot profit nor deliver; for they are vain. For the LORD will not forsake his people for his great name's sake: because it hath pleased the LORD to make you his people. Guidance: The thunder works. The people who were so confident about their political demand are now begging Samuel not to let them die. They name their request a 'sin' for the first time, and they ask the right person to pray. Samuel's response is one of the most pastoral lines in the Old Testament: 'Fear not.' Yes, you have sinned. No, you do not need to despair. The remedy is not panic but renewed obedience: 'turn not aside… serve the LORD with all your heart.' Then comes a sentence to memorize: 'The LORD will not forsake his people for his great name's sake.' Your security does not rest on the cleanness of your record, but on the costliness of His reputation. He has staked His name on you; He will not abandon what He has named. This is the gospel logic before the gospel — God keeps His people because His name is wrapped up in them, not because they have earned the keeping.

Moreover as for me, God forbid that I should sin against the LORD in ceasing to pray for you: but I will teach you the good and the right way: Only fear the LORD, and serve him in truth with all your heart: for consider how great things he hath done for you. But if ye shall still do wickedly, ye shall be consumed, both ye and your king. Guidance: One of the great pastoral confessions in scripture: 'God forbid that I should sin against the LORD in ceasing to pray for you.' Samuel categorizes failing to intercede for his people as a sin against God — not just a kindness withheld, but a duty refused. He sees prayer not as optional spiritual hobby but as obligation. He is stepping down from political leadership, but not from intercession. The transition does not retire him from the priestly bench. He frames his ongoing ministry as two parts: 'pray for you' and 'teach you the good and the right way.' Intercession and instruction — the two timeless tasks every faithful elder owes a community. He closes with a sober warning: do not test the mercy. The covenant holds, but it is not indifference. The king does not exempt the nation from consequences; if anything, he doubles them — 'both ye and your king.' Faith ends the chapter neither sentimental nor cynical, but soberly hopeful.
